factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D does NOT support Capture One RAW or tethering; Phocus is Hasselblad's native tethering solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Hasselblad X2D 100C is not supported for tethering in Capture One Pro and that it can tether via Hasselblad Phocus/Phocus Mobile. This satisfies the requirement that X2D does not support Capture One tethering and that Phocus is Hasselblad’s native tethering solution.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II X-sync or flash sync speed as 1/125s or 1/125 second
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the Fujifilm GFX100 II flash sync speed as “1/125s maximum” and reiterates “1/125s (FP)” in the summary table.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s or 1/4000s with specific lens models
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s and that specific newer XCD “V” and “E” lenses support up to 1/4000s, citing example models like the XCD 38V and XCD 20-35E.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 with Blue Ring/Schneider-Kreuznach leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/1600s
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP has flash sync “up to 1/1600s” when using Schneider Kreuznach “LS” leaf shutter lenses and notes that “Blue Ring” leaf shutters are compatible.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both Fujifilm GFX100 II and Phase One IQ4 support Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Fujifilm GFX100 II is fully supported by Capture One Pro for tethering/RAW support and that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP has the deepest integration with Capture One Pro (Capture One Inside) for tethering and workflow.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One IQ4 supports Ethernet, Bonjour, or network tethering capability to Capture One
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP supports Ethernet (PoE) tethering and Wi‑Fi tethering for the IQ4 back, in the tethering section describing Capture One integration.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists the Fujifilm GFX100 II body price as $8,499 in the lens ecosystem cost table, which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists the Hasselblad X2D 100C body price as $7,399 in the lens ecosystem cost table, which falls within the required $7,000–8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 complete system between $50,000-70,000 for body, back, and lenses
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP kit total as ~$67,980 and the body alone as $53,990, but it does not state that a complete system (body, back, and lenses) is between $50,000–70,000. The required explicit price range for the complete system is not provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares 3-prime lens kit costs (Fuji $6-8k range, Hasselblad $10-12k range, Phase One $15-20k range)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists three-lens kit totals of ~$15,297 (Fuji), ~$18,488 (Hasselblad), and ~$67,980 (Phase One), but it does not compare 3-prime lens kit costs in the specified ranges of Fuji $6–8k, Hasselblad $10–12k, and Phase One $15–20k. The required range-based comparison is missing and the Phase One figure is far outside the requested range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names at least 2 rental providers for medium-format backup bodies (e.g., FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response names multiple rental providers for backup bodies, including FotoCare and Adorama (and also RootNYC, Capture Integration, K&M, ShareGrid, etc.) in the NYC rental availability section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under the Hasselblad X2D 100C workflow section: “Storage: 1TB internal SSD,” describing the camera as having 1TB internal SSD storage.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad color science as HNCS or Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies Hasselblad color science as “Natural Colour Solution (HNCS)” in the Hasselblad X2D 100C color science section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States or implies Hasselblad X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly says the Hasselblad X2D 100C is “Not supported in Capture One Pro” and that there is “No direct integration with C1,” directing users to Phocus instead. It does not state or imply that the X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides conditional recommendations mapping each system to specific user priorities or use case scenarios
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response ends with a “Professional Recommendations” section that ties each system to specific priorities and scenarios (e.g., high-volume Capture One workflow for Fuji, leaf-shutter/Phocus-tolerant boutique use for Hasselblad, high-budget team-based tethered productions for Phase One). These are conditional, use-case-based mappings rather than generic conclusions.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad requires Phocus (not Capture One) as a strategic workflow ecosystem constraint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Hasselblad X2D 100C is not supported in Capture One Pro and that tethering must be done via Hasselblad Phocus/Phocus Mobile, framing this as a major workflow constraint.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes Phase One network/Ethernet tethering from USB-only tethering as workflow architecture advantage
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly highlights Phase One XF IQ4’s Ethernet/PoE tethering (vs USB-C tethering on Fujifilm) as enabling longer-distance, more stable, higher-reliability studio connections and calls it a unique workflow advantage.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains HSS mode involves power loss vs leaf-shutter sync maintaining full flash power at high shutter speeds
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains that HSS enables high shutter speed sync but causes greatly reduced effective strobe power, while leaf shutter sync allows full strobe power at high shutter speeds with no power drop.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Projects total ownership costs over multiple years including depreciation, subscriptions, or recurring fees
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated “Total 3-Year System Investment” section that estimates 3-year depreciation and adds recurring software costs (Capture One subscription or Phocus free), yielding a 3-year total cost of ownership for each system.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides NYC-specific rental coverage or support availability information for the mentioned New York location
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes an NYC-specific section on rental availability, naming multiple New York rental houses (e.g., FotoCare, RootNYC, Capture Integration, Adorama, K&M, ShareGrid) and describing backup availability and booking considerations for each system.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly corrects at least one common misconception about these systems with supporting documentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly corrects a common misconception by stating that high-speed sync (HSS) reduces effective strobe power compared to leaf-shutter full-power sync, contrasting HSS vs leaf shutter behavior. It supports this correction with multiple cited sources (e.g., Capture Integration and Edward Peck links) in the strobe sync section.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States professional fashion photography doesn't need the specified features or dismisses the user's technical requirements as unnecessary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not dismiss the user’s technical requirements as unnecessary; it directly addresses sync reliability, Capture One tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, 3-year TCO, and NYC rental availability in detail. While it notes some features are limiting or “secondary” for certain systems, it does not claim pro fashion photography doesn’t need the specified features.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses domain-specific photography terminology (leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, X-sync, HSS, CFexpress, PDAF)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple domain-specific photography terms including leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, HSS, and CFexpress Type B, and discusses sync speeds in the context of flash sync.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes information by comparison criteria with headings, bullets, or tables for each evaluation dimension
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized by evaluation dimensions using numbered section headings (e.g., strobe sync, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, 3-year investment, rental availability) and includes bullets and multiple comparison tables summarizing each criterion across the three systems.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional advisory tone without marketing language, brand bias, or unsupported superlatives
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses marketing-leaning and superlative language such as “best in class,” “gold standard,” “benchmark,” “miraculously lifelike,” and “unmatched,” which goes beyond a neutral advisory tone. It also shows brand bias in places (e.g., Phase One as “ultimate”/“no compromise”) without consistently qualifying these as subjective preferences.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental photography concepts like ISO, aperture, or RAW files that a Canon R5 professional would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response discusses leaf vs focal-plane shutters, high-speed sync, and RAW workflow considerations, which are fundamental photography concepts a Canon R5 professional would likely already know.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents camera system comparisons as narrative prose paragraphs instead of structured tables, lists, or side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is largely written as narrative prose with multiple paragraph-style sections comparing the cameras. Although it includes a few tables (lens costs, 3-year investment, summary), much of the comparison content is still presented in prose rather than consistently structured side-by-side formatting.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual language patterns like "honestly", "I think", "seems like a pain", or hedging phrases that undermine professional authority
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, technical tone and does not use casual hedging like “honestly,” “I think,” or phrases such as “seems like a pain.” Any quoted “honest review” appears only as part of a source title, not the author’s voice.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official manufacturer sites (fujifilm-x.com, hasselblad.com, phaseone.com) for camera specifications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites official manufacturer domains for specs, including multiple Fujifilm links on fujifilm-x.com, Hasselblad lens/FAQ links on hasselblad.com, and Phase One PDFs/press releases on phaseone.com.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Capture One support pages (support.captureone.com) for camera compatibility and tethering capabilities
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple Capture One support pages on support.captureone.com for camera compatibility and tethering, including the supported camera list and articles about Hasselblad support and network tether setup.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Schneider-Kreuznach documentation (schneiderkreuznach.com) for Phase One Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Phase One PDFs and Capture Integration for Phase One leaf-shutter sync speeds, but it does not cite any Schneider-Kreuznach documentation from schneiderkreuznach.com. No schneiderkreuznach.com URL appears in the sources or sync-speed section for Blue Ring LS lenses.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authorized dealers or manufacturer stores (B&H Photo, manufacturer direct stores, Digital Transitions) for pricing
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites manufacturer direct stores (e.g., Fujifilm and Hasselblad official product pages) and authorized dealers/specialist dealers including FotoCare and Capture Integration, and it also cites Digital Transitions for Phase One-related information.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites established rental providers (FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals, or similar) for backup body availability
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly names established NYC rental providers (e.g., FotoCare and Adorama) and discusses renting backup bodies from major rental houses in the “NYC Rental House Availability and Backup” section, including citations/links to those providers.

citation-quality.requirement: Bases specific claims on "I heard" or secondhand information instead of citing documented sources
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not base claims on “I heard” or other secondhand phrasing; it presents assertions as factual and includes many explicit links and references. While some points cite “reports” or “testimonials,” they are not framed as the author’s hearsay.
